reasoning: The wp_ajax_plt_dismiss_notice handler (line 158/942-946) has exactly one gate: WordPress authentication cookie required by the wp_ajax_ hook prefix (not wp_ajax_nopriv_). There is no nonce, no capability check, and no secret value. The gate value (a valid WordPress session cookie) is trivially possessed by ANY logged-in user — subscriber, customer, contributor — in a default WordPress install. The exploitation path requires only: (1) be logged in to the site as any user role, (2) send POST /wp-admin/admin-ajax.php?action=plt_dismiss_notice&plt_notice=4 with no additional tokens. The handler calls dismiss_notice() which calls update_option('page_links_dismissed_options', ...) — a site-wide persistent option, not per-user meta — permanently suppressing admin notices for ALL users. CSRF is also present (no nonce), meaning a logged-in admin browsing a malicious page could be forced to trigger it cross-site, but the broken_access_control framing is primary. Severity remains medium: impact is site-wide persistent state corruption (suppressing admin notices for all users, including security/update alerts), not code execution or data exfiltration. Comparison: ajax_quick_add (line 953-969) demonstrates the developer knew how to secure AJAX handlers — it calls current_user_can('edit_pages') AND check_ajax_referer('plt-quick-add', 'plt_nonce') — confirming this omission on ajax_dismiss_notice is a genuine oversight, not intentional open access.
